title: The Case for Code-Driven Reporting
text: Methodology Paper · 12 min read

title: The Case for Code-Driven Reporting
text:
The problem nobody quite admits
Every analytics team I’ve worked, across financial services, pharma measurement, and ad-tech — has the a similar weekly routine. An analyst is hunched over a PowerPoint, copy-pasting numbers from Excel into a deck because the executive review is at 2 PM and the data refreshed at 11. Someone else is in Slack asking “wait, which version of the Q3 file is the real one?” A senior analyst is fixing a formula in a workbook that breaks every quarter when the date format shifts.
This is normal. It’s also a slow-motion disaster.
I’m going to make a strong claim: manually assembled deliverables are the single biggest source of avoidable risk and waste in modern analytics teams. Bigger than bad SQL. Bigger than tooling debates. Bigger than headcount. And yet most teams treat the problem as a soft cultural issue (“we should automate more”) rather than what it actually is: a structural choice with measurable cost.
This essay lays out the case for code-driven reporting — treating analytical deliverables the way software engineers treat applications. It’s not a tools post. It’s an argument about what analytics work is, and what it should be.

title: The Case for Code-Driven Reporting
text:
What “code-driven reporting” actually means
The term is broader than it sounds. I don’t just mean “use Python instead of Excel.” I mean a specific commitment:

Every analytical deliverable, every chart, table, metric, and narrative - should be reproducible from raw data with a single command.
The single-command test for analytics maturity.

If your team cannot explain where a number came from, what logic was applied, and how to reproduce it through a defined script or workflow, then the process is not mature analytics. It is handcrafted reporting. That has a place (research, exploration) but it shouldn’t be the output medium for recurring deliverables.
Code-driven reporting means:

Inputs are explicit. Source data is specified, dated, and pulled from a system of record, not “the file Sarah sent.”
Logic is in code. Filtering, aggregation, business rules, and metric definitions live in version controlled files, not in someone’s head or a hidden Excel formula.
Outputs are generated. The PDF, HTML report, Excel workbook, or slide deck is produced by a script that anyone on the team can run.
Changes are tracked. When the CFO asks “why did this number change from last quarter?” the answer is a git diff, not a memory test.
Reviews happen on the code. Pull requests force methodology discussions before reports go out.

That’s the full commitment. Anything less is a halfway state that incurs most of the cost of manual work without much of the benefit of automation.

title: The Case for Code-Driven Reporting
text:
The maturity model
Most teams sit somewhere on this spectrum. Be honest about where yours actually is.

Level 0: Manual/Artisanal reporting
Reports are assembled by hand each cycle. Numbers come from running queries, copying outputs, screenshots, and pasting into PowerPoint or Excel. Senior analysts become bottlenecks because they are often the only ones who know how each metric is calculated.
Cost signal: When someone leaves the team, reporting capability degrades for weeks.


Level 1: Templated reporting
A spreadsheet or PowerPoint template exists. Data still gets pasted in manually, but at least the formatting is consistent. Charts may update from source ranges, but the process still depends on someone moving the data into place.
Cost signal: Quarter end still takes three or more days of analyst time, every time.


Level 2: Scripted extraction
Scripts pull data from source systems and produce intermediate outputs like CSVs, summary tables, or refreshed datasets. This is progress, but a human still has to assemble, validate, and explain the final deliverable.
Cost signal: Reports go out, but the team still has to manually investigate what changed and why.


Level 3: Parameterized reporting
A Quarto, R Markdown, Python, Jupyter notebook, or similar workflow can render the full deliverable from raw data. Parameters control the client, date range, market, channel, or scope. One clear process produces the final artifact.
Cost signal: New report versions take hours, not days.


Level 4: Continuous reporting
Reports run automatically on a schedule or when the data refreshes. Outputs are versioned, archived, and easy to access. Methodology changes go through review. Basic checks catch broken logic, missing data, or unexpected changes before stakeholders see the output.
Cost signal: The team spends most of its time answering new analytical questions, not recreating the same reports over and over.
Most teams I have seen, including good ones, sit at Level 1 or Level 2 and think they are at Level 3.
The honest test is simple: pick last quarter’s executive deck. Can someone who was not involved in the original work reproduce it from the raw data this morning and explain where the numbers came from before lunch?
If the answer is no, the team is below Level 3.

title: The Case for Code-Driven Reporting
text:
Why teams stay stuck
The objections are predictable. They’re also mostly wrong.
“We don’t have time to build infrastructure.” This is the most common objection and it’s exactly backwards. The time you don’t have is being consumed by the manual work itself. The investment pays back in weeks for any recurring report. The math is brutal: a 40-hour build that saves 4 hours/week breaks even in 10 weeks and saves 200+ hours/year thereafter. I have never seen a team where the math didn’t work.
“Our stakeholders want PowerPoint.” They want the information in a format they know how to read. They do not care how the PowerPoint was made.
A scripted workflow can still produce a PowerPoint file. The difference is that the deck can have consistent formatting, accurate numbers, and a clear trail back to the source data and logic.
The stakeholder gets the format they want. The analytics team gets a process that does not depend on manual rebuilding every cycle.
“Our data is too messy.” Yes. That is exactly why the cleaning logic needs to be documented and stored in code.
Messy data handled manually is where mistakes spread quietly. Messy data handled through repeatable logic is where upstream issues get caught earlier.
The point is not that the data has to be perfect. The point is that the logic should not live only in someone’s memory.
“We’re not engineers.” You do not need to be a software engineer to make reporting reproducible.
Tools like Quarto, R Markdown, Jupyter, dbt, Python, R, and SQL are built for this kind of work. Analysts do not need to turn into engineers, but they do need to take the process seriously.
If a report goes out every week, every month, or every quarter, the workflow should be treated like something the business depends on, because it does.
“It’s not worth it for one-off analyses.” Agreed.
Not every analysis needs a full process around it. Exploration can be messy. Research can be flexible. Not every question deserves a pipeline.
The discipline applies for reacurrent deliverables.
If the same report, metric, deck, or analysis keeps coming back, then it should not keep being rebuilt from scratch.

title: The Case for Code-Driven Reporting
section: What it costs to stay at Level 1
text:
What it costs to stay at Level 1
I want to put real numbers around this because the cost of manual reporting is easy to ignore until someone measures it.
A team I worked with had five analysts and one director. Each month, the team produced 12 recurring client reports plus ad hoc work. Manual assembly took about 6 hours per report, plus another 2 hours of senior review.
That is 96 hours per month spent on production work alone.Across the team, that was roughly 30% of total analyst capacity going to producing things that had been produced 50+ times before
For the people most involved in the reporting cycle, that represented a meaningful share of available reporting capacity. Time that could have gone toward actual analysis was being spent rebuilding deliverables that had already been produced dozens of times before.
After moving the recurring reports to a code driven pipeline using Quarto, parameterized R, and a scheduler, production time dropped to about 4 hours per month for the same 12 reports.
The reclaimed time went back into actual analytical work: improving methodology, building better measurement frameworks, investigating performance changes, and having deeper client conversations.
The build took two analysts about six weeks of partial effort.
It saved roughly 1,100 analyst hours per year.
That is more than half of a full time analyst recovered from one project, with the savings continuing every year after.
This is not some rare outcome. For recurring reporting, this is usually what happens when the process is rebuilt the right way.

title: The Case for Code-Driven Reporting
section: What “doing it right” looks like in practice
text:
What “doing it right” looks like in practice
Here’s the structure I converge on, regardless of language:
project/
├── data/                # Raw data (gitignored if large)
├── Python/R/ or src/    # Cleaning, transformation, modeling functions
├── reports/             # Quarto/RMarkdown/Jupyter sources
│   ├── _quarto.yml      # Site/book config
│   ├── monthly_review.qmd
│   └── client_X_report.qmd
├── output/              # Rendered deliverables (gitignored)
├── tests/               # Tests for transformation functions
├── .venv/renv.lock or pyproject.toml   # Pinned dependencies
└── README.md            # How to run it
The non-negotiables:

Pinned dependencies. renv for R, uv/pyproject.toml for Python. The pipeline must run identically on a teammate’s laptop a year from now.
Functions live separately from reports. The .qmd calls functions; it doesn’t define them. This makes everything testable.
Tests on transformations, not reports. Test that compute_cpa() returns the right number for known inputs. Don’t test the rendered HTML.
Parameterization is first-class. Reports take parameters (date range, client ID). Variants are configuration, not copies.
Outputs are reproducible from main. The current branch should always produce a runnable pipeline.

title: The Case for Code-Driven Reporting
text:
The leadership angle
This isn’t a tooling argument. It’s a leadership argument.
Analytics managers and directors who insist on code-driven reporting do three things at once:
They protect their team from burnout. Manual deliverable production is the work analysts complain about most. It’s repetitive, error-prone, and high-stakes. Eliminating it makes the job better.
They protect the business from quiet errors.
A mistake in a manually built deck can sit there for weeks before anyone notices. A copied number, a stale chart, or a missed filter can make its way into a client meeting or executive review.
A repeatable workflow does not remove every risk, but it gives the team a better chance to catch issues earlier. Logic can be reviewed. Outputs can be checked. Changes can be traced back to the source.
They build a team that scales. A team at Level 3 or higher can take on more work without needing headcount to grow at the same rate. A team stuck at Level 1 needs more analyst time every time the reporting volume increases.
That is not a sustainable operating model. It turns talented analysts into report producers instead of problem solvers.

The best analytics leaders I know do not measure their teams by the number of decks produced. They measure by the number of recurring deliverables that no longer require manual assembly.
A useful KPI for analytics leadership.

If you are an analytics leader and your team is at Level 1 or Level 2, you have a budget problem. You are paying for it in analyst time.
If you are an analytics individual contributor and your team is at Level 1 or Level 2, you have a career problem. Your most valuable hours are being spent on work that does not build your skills, improve the business, or compound over time.

title: The Case for Code-Driven Reporting
text:
Where to start
If you’re convinced and want to move, here’s the sequence that has worked for me:

Pick one recurring report. Not the most complex. Pick one that runs at least monthly, has a stable structure, and would be embarrassing if it broke.
Reproduce it in Quarto or RMarkdown. Match the existing format exactly. The goal is not to improve the report — it’s to demonstrate that the code can produce the same output.
Run both side-by-side for one cycle. Generate the manual version and the automated version. Check that they match. Fix the discrepancies.
Switch over. The next cycle, only run the automated version. Keep the manual process as fallback for one more cycle, then retire it.
Repeat. Move the next report. Then the next. Within a year you’ll have inverted the team’s time distribution.

The hardest part is the first one. The seventh report takes 1/4 the time of the first. The team builds shared infrastructure as they go.

title: The Case for Code-Driven Reporting
text:
Closing
Code-driven reporting is not a fashion choice or a technical preference. It’s a strategic decision about how an analytics team allocates its scarcest resource — analyst time — and how it manages its largest hidden risk: silent errors in artisanal work.
The teams that take this seriously will spend their time on harder, more valuable problems. The teams that don’t will keep producing decks until automation does it for them. There is no third option.

title: Automated Reporting Pipeline
text:
The Business Problem
Recurring reports are where analytics teams quietly bleed hours. A monthly client deliverable that takes 6 hours to assemble, times 12 months, times multiple clients, that adds up to real money and nobody budgets for it.
The CMS Open Payments dataset is a useful proving ground because it has the same qualities that make real client work hard:

Real volume, millions of records per program year
Real messiness, the same parent company files under a pile of legal entity names, payment type labels run to 140 characters
Real reporting needs, journalists and compliance teams actually publish off this data

The goal: one command produces a publication quality summary report, HTML and Excel, for any manufacturer in the dataset, ready to send to a stakeholder.

title: Automated Reporting Pipeline
text:
The Approach



1


Modular cleaning functions
Cleaning logic lives in standalone R functions, not buried in the report. Each function does one thing and is independently testable.




2


Parameterized Quarto report
The report template takes parameters, company name and reporting period. One template renders for any company.




3


Publication quality tables (gt)
gt and gtExtras for tables that look like they belong in a Wall Street Journal feature, not a notebook dump.




4


Excel output via openxlsx2
Same data, formatted Excel workbook with frozen panes and multiple sheets, for the stakeholders who live in Excel. Which is most of them.

title: Automated Reporting Pipeline
section: Step 1: Load CMS Open Payments data
text:
Step 1: Load CMS Open Payments data
The fetch script (data/fetch_cms.py) downloads the General Payments bulk file from download.cms.gov/openpayments/, filters it to the eight target manufacturers with a Polars lazy scan, and writes Parquet. CMS rotates the bulk filename on every refresh so the script takes the current URL as --url, the README has the details.
If the cache is missing (you cloned the repo and haven’t fetched yet) the chunk falls back to a clearly labeled synthetic dataset with the same schema so the page still renders. The synthetic path is only a fallback, the goal is real data and everything below was rendered against it.


Load CMS Open Payments — real if cached, synthetic fallback otherwise
library(arrow)
library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)
library(lubridate)
library(stringr)

cache_path <- file.path("..", "data", "cache", "cms", "general_payments.parquet")
using_real_data <- file.exists(cache_path)

if (using_real_data) {
  ## the cache already uses short friendly names. rename them BACK to the
  ## real CMS bulk header names so the cleaning functions below face the
  ## same schema the raw file throws at you in production
  payments_raw <- read_parquet(cache_path) %>%
    rename(
      applicable_manufacturer_or_applicable_gpo_making_payment_name = company,
      physician_specialty                                            = specialty,
      recipient_state                                                = state,
      date_of_payment                                                = payment_date,
      nature_of_payment_or_transfer_of_value                         = payment_type,
      total_amount_of_payment_usdollars                              = amount_usd
    )
  cat("source: REAL CMS Open Payments (filtered to 8 manufacturers via fetch_cms.py)\n")
} else {
  cat("source: SYNTHETIC fallback (CMS cache not found at ", cache_path, ")\n", sep = "")
  cat("  -> run `python data/fetch_cms.py --url <current-bulk-url>` for real data.\n\n")
  ## same schema as the real file, obviously fake numbers
  set.seed(2024)
  n_records <- 8000
  companies <- c(
    "Pfizer Inc.", "Johnson & Johnson", "Merck & Co.",
    "AbbVie Inc.", "Eli Lilly and Company", "Bristol-Myers Squibb",
    "Novartis Pharmaceuticals", "GlaxoSmithKline LLC"
  )
  specialties <- c(
    "Internal Medicine", "Cardiology", "Oncology", "Family Medicine",
    "Psychiatry", "Neurology", "Endocrinology", "Dermatology",
    "Orthopedic Surgery", "Anesthesiology"
  )
  states <- c("CA","TX","NY","FL","IL","PA","OH","GA","NC","MI",
              "NJ","VA","WA","AZ","MA","TN","IN","MO","MD","WI")
  payment_types <- c(
    "Compensation for services other than consulting" = 0.12,
    "Consulting Fee" = 0.18, "Food and Beverage" = 0.32,
    "Travel and Lodging" = 0.10, "Education" = 0.08,
    "Honoraria" = 0.06, "Grant" = 0.04, "Royalty or License" = 0.02,
    "Charitable Contribution" = 0.03, "Gift" = 0.05
  )
  payments_raw <- tibble(
    record_id = seq_len(n_records),
    applicable_manufacturer_or_applicable_gpo_making_payment_name = sample(
      companies, n_records, replace = TRUE,
      prob = c(0.18, 0.16, 0.14, 0.12, 0.11, 0.10, 0.10, 0.09)
    ),
    physician_specialty = sample(specialties, n_records, replace = TRUE),
    recipient_state = sample(states, n_records, replace = TRUE),
    date_of_payment = sample(
      seq.Date(as.Date("2024-01-01"), as.Date("2024-12-31"), by = "day"),
      n_records, replace = TRUE
    ),
    nature_of_payment_or_transfer_of_value = sample(
      names(payment_types), n_records, replace = TRUE,
      prob = unname(payment_types)
    )
  ) %>%
    mutate(
      total_amount_of_payment_usdollars = round(rlnorm(n(), meanlog = 4.8, sdlog = 1.6), 2),
      recipient_state = ifelse(runif(n()) < 0.015, NA_character_, recipient_state),
      physician_specialty = ifelse(runif(n()) < 0.01, NA_character_, physician_specialty)
    )
  ### sprinkle in duplicate filings so the dedupe step has something to do
  dupe_rows <- payments_raw %>% slice_sample(n = 150)
  payments_raw <- bind_rows(payments_raw, dupe_rows)
}

## quick honesty check on the storage story. write the exact same rows back
## out as csv, measure both files, delete the csv. takes about two seconds
## and the key results section reads this number at render time
if (using_real_data) {
  tmp_csv <- tempfile(fileext = ".csv")
  write_csv_arrow(payments_raw, tmp_csv)
  compression_ratio <- file.size(tmp_csv) / file.size(cache_path)
  cat(sprintf("on disk: %.0f MB as csv vs %.0f MB as parquet, %.1fx smaller\n",
      file.size(tmp_csv) / 1024^2, file.size(cache_path) / 1024^2, compression_ratio))
  unlink(tmp_csv)
} else {
  compression_ratio <- NA
}

title: Automated Reporting Pipeline
text:
Step 2: Modular cleaning functions
The cleaning lives in standalone functions, in a separate R/ directory in production. Each one is testable on its own and the report just calls the pipeline.
The one worth a second look is roll_up_company(). The raw file lists 26 different payer names for what a stakeholder would call 7 companies. “PFIZER INC.” and “PFIZER INTERNATIONAL LLC” are not two companies, and Janssen is just Johnson & Johnson wearing a different badge. Skip this step and every company level number in the report is wrong.


Modular cleaning functions (would live in R/clean.R in production)
## rename the wall-of-text CMS bulk columns to names a human can type
clean_column_names <- function(df) {
  df %>%
    rename(
      company       = applicable_manufacturer_or_applicable_gpo_making_payment_name,
      specialty     = physician_specialty,
      state         = recipient_state,
      payment_date  = date_of_payment,
      payment_type  = nature_of_payment_or_transfer_of_value,
      amount_usd    = total_amount_of_payment_usdollars
    )
}

## drop duplicate filings, keep the first occurrence
deduplicate_records <- function(df) {
  df %>% distinct(record_id, .keep_all = TRUE)
}

## missing values get documented defaults instead of silent NA drops
handle_missing <- function(df) {
  df %>%
    mutate(
      state = if_else(is.na(state), "Unknown", state),
      specialty = if_else(is.na(specialty), "Not Reported", specialty)
    )
}

## the same parent files under a pile of legal entities. roll them up so
## company level numbers mean what a reader thinks they mean
### same entity resolution problem as the FEC vendor names in the political
### spend study, just smaller. 26 names here, so a checked-by-hand regex
### map is enough. at vendor scale you graduate to fuzzy matching
roll_up_company <- function(df) {
  df %>%
    mutate(parent = case_when(
      str_detect(str_to_upper(company), "ABBVIE")          ~ "AbbVie",
      str_detect(str_to_upper(company), "PFIZER")          ~ "Pfizer",
      str_detect(str_to_upper(company), "NOVARTIS")        ~ "Novartis",
      str_detect(str_to_upper(company), "GLAXO|GSK")       ~ "GSK",
      str_detect(str_to_upper(company), "JANSSEN|JOHNSON") ~ "Johnson & Johnson",
      str_detect(str_to_upper(company), "MERCK")           ~ "Merck",
      str_detect(str_to_upper(company), "LILLY")           ~ "Eli Lilly",
      str_detect(str_to_upper(company), "BRISTOL")         ~ "Bristol Myers Squibb",
      TRUE ~ company   ## anything unmapped keeps its raw name so it's visible
    ))
}

## CMS payment type labels run up to 140 characters. nobody wants that in
## a table row, and the longest one is also the biggest dollar bucket
shorten_payment_types <- function(df) {
  df %>%
    mutate(payment_type_short = case_when(
      str_detect(payment_type, "^Compensation for services other than consulting") ~ "Speaker / services fees",
      str_detect(payment_type, "^Compensation for serving as faculty")             ~ "CME speaker fees",
      payment_type == "Food and Beverage"   ~ "Meals",
      payment_type == "Consulting Fee"      ~ "Consulting",
      payment_type == "Travel and Lodging"  ~ "Travel and lodging",
      payment_type == "Royalty or License"  ~ "Royalties",
      str_detect(payment_type, "^Space rental")            ~ "Facility fees",
      str_detect(payment_type, "^Long term medical supply") ~ "Device loans",
      TRUE ~ payment_type
    ))
}

## flag outliers with 3x IQR inside each payment type. flag, never remove.
### a $22,000 royalty is normal, a $22,000 meal is not, that's why the
### threshold is computed per payment type and not globally
flag_outliers <- function(df, multiplier = 3) {
  df %>%
    group_by(payment_type) %>%
    mutate(
      .q1 = quantile(amount_usd, 0.25, na.rm = TRUE),
      .q3 = quantile(amount_usd, 0.75, na.rm = TRUE),
      is_outlier = amount_usd > .q3 + multiplier * (.q3 - .q1)
    ) %>%
    ungroup() %>%
    select(-.q1, -.q3)
}

## derived time dimensions, every report downstream wants these
add_time_dimensions <- function(df) {
  df %>%
    mutate(
      year = year(payment_date),
      quarter = paste0("Q", quarter(payment_date)),
      month = month(payment_date, label = TRUE, abbr = TRUE),
      year_month = floor_date(payment_date, "month")
    )
}

## the full pipeline reads like the run sheet
clean_pipeline <- function(df) {
  df %>%
    clean_column_names() %>%
    deduplicate_records() %>%
    handle_missing() %>%
    roll_up_company() %>%
    shorten_payment_types() %>%
    flag_outliers() %>%
    add_time_dimensions()
}

payments <- clean_pipeline(payments_raw)

## the key results section reads these at render time, no hand-typed numbers
n_raw_names <- n_distinct(payments$company)
n_parents   <- n_distinct(payments$parent)
n_dupes     <- nrow(payments_raw) - nrow(payments)
pct_outliers <- mean(payments$is_outlier) * 100

fmt_n <- function(n) if (n >= 1e6) sprintf("%.1fM", n / 1e6) else format(n, big.mark = ",")

cat("after cleaning:\n")


after cleaning:


Modular cleaning functions (would live in R/clean.R in production)
cat("  records:   ", format(nrow(payments), big.mark = ","), "\n")


  records:    3,774,874 


Modular cleaning functions (would live in R/clean.R in production)
cat("  dupes out: ", format(n_dupes, big.mark = ","), "\n")


  dupes out:  0 


Modular cleaning functions (would live in R/clean.R in production)
cat("  outliers:  ", format(sum(payments$is_outlier), big.mark = ","),
    sprintf(" (%.1f%%, flagged not removed)\n", pct_outliers))


  outliers:   156,425  (4.1%, flagged not removed)

title: Automated Reporting Pipeline
section: Step 3: Publication quality tables with gt
text:
Step 3: Publication quality tables with gt
This is where R earns its keep. gt produces tables that genuinely belong in a published report. The summary excludes flagged outliers on purpose, they get their own sheet in the Excel workbook later so nothing disappears, it just gets labeled.


Build publication-quality summary table with gt
library(gt)
library(scales)

## top spend parent in the cleaned data becomes the parameterized example.
## resolved at run time, not hard coded, because the ranking is allowed to
## change when the data refreshes
target_company <- payments %>%
  filter(!is_outlier) %>%
  count(parent, wt = amount_usd, name = "spend") %>%
  slice_max(spend, n = 1) %>%
  pull(parent)

company_summary <- payments %>%
  filter(parent == target_company, !is_outlier) %>%
  group_by(payment_type_short) %>%
  summarise(
    n_payments = n(),
    total_amount = sum(amount_usd),
    median_amount = median(amount_usd),
    avg_amount = mean(amount_usd),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  arrange(desc(total_amount)) %>%
  mutate(pct_of_total = total_amount / sum(total_amount))

summary_year <- year(min(payments$payment_date, na.rm = TRUE))
company_summary %>%
  gt() %>%
  tab_header(
    title = md(paste0("**", target_company, " — Payment Summary**")),
    subtitle = paste0("Open Payments General Payments · Program Year ", summary_year,
                      " · outliers excluded")
  ) %>%
  fmt_currency(columns = c(total_amount, median_amount, avg_amount), decimals = 0) %>%
  fmt_number(columns = n_payments, decimals = 0, sep_mark = ",") %>%

title: Automated Reporting Pipeline
text:
Where the money actually goes
The table is the deliverable, but the chart is what gets remembered. Same rules as the other case studies: the title states the finding, labels sit on the data instead of a legend, one idea per chart.
And the finding here is a good one. Count the payments and this dataset is a catering ledger, nearly every record is a meal with an $18 median. Count the dollars and the meals barely matter, the money is speaker fees and consulting.


Shared chart style
library(ggplot2)

## same palette as the rest of the portfolio. navy carries the money story,
## slate fades the supporting series into the background
NAVY <- "#0f172a"; BLUE <- "#2563eb"; SLATE <- "#94a3b8"
GRAY <- "#64748b"; GREEN <- "#059669"; AMBER <- "#d97706"

theme_exec <- function() {
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
    theme(
      plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 14, color = NAVY),
      plot.subtitle = element_text(color = GRAY, size = 9.5),
      plot.caption = element_text(color = GRAY, size = 8.5),
      plot.title.position = "plot",
      panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
      legend.position = "none"
    )
}




Share of payment count vs share of dollars by payment type
## five buckets tell the story, everything else is rounding error
mix <- payments %>%
  mutate(bucket = case_when(
    payment_type_short %in% c("Speaker / services fees", "CME speaker fees") ~ "Speaker fees",
    payment_type_short %in% c("Meals", "Consulting", "Travel and lodging")   ~ payment_type_short,
    TRUE ~ "All other"
  )) %>%
  group_by(bucket) %>%
  summarise(n = n(), dollars = sum(amount_usd), .groups = "drop") %>%
  mutate(
    pct_n = n / sum(n) * 100,
    pct_d = dollars / sum(dollars) * 100
  )

## order by dollars so the money story reads top down
mix <- mix %>% arrange(pct_d)
mix$bucket <- factor(mix$bucket, levels = mix$bucket)

long <- mix %>%
  pivot_longer(c(pct_d, pct_n), names_to = "measure", values_to = "pct") %>%
  mutate(measure = factor(measure, levels = c("pct_n", "pct_d")))

### the top group's labels name the series, so no legend box needed
top_bucket <- levels(mix$bucket)[nrow(mix)]
long <- long %>%
  mutate(lab = case_when(
    bucket == top_bucket & measure == "pct_d" ~ sprintf("%.0f%% of the dollars", pct),
    bucket == top_bucket & measure == "pct_n" ~ sprintf("%.0f%% of the payments", pct),
    pct < 1   ~ sprintf("%.1f%%", pct),
    TRUE      ~ sprintf("%.0f%%", pct)
  ))

ggplot(long, aes(x = pct, y = bucket, fill = measure)) +
  geom_col(position = position_dodge(width = 0.72), width = 0.62) +
  geom_text(aes(label = lab, color = measure),
            position = position_dodge(width = 0.72),
            hjust = -0.12, size = 3.4, fontface = "bold") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c(pct_d = NAVY, pct_n = SLATE)) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c(pct_d = NAVY, pct_n = SLATE)) +
  scale_x_continuous(limits = c(0, 118), expand = c(0, 0)) +
  labs(
    title = "Almost every payment is a meal. Almost none of the money is",
    subtitle = sprintf("share of record count vs share of dollars by payment type, CMS program year %d, %d manufacturers", summary_year, n_parents),
    x = NULL, y = NULL
  ) +
  theme_exec() +
  theme(
    axis.text.x = element_blank(),       ## labels carry the values, axis can go
    panel.grid.major.x = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.major.y = element_blank(),
    axis.text.y = element_text(size = 10, color = NAVY)
  )





Share of payment records vs share of dollars by payment type, all manufacturers combined. The median meal is $18, the median speaker fee is $2,345.




This is the chart a compliance officer or a journalist actually needs. Headlines about pharma money usually count payments, which means they’re mostly counting sandwiches. The dollars sit somewhere else entirely.

title: Automated Reporting Pipeline
section: One company is half the money, every month
text:
One company is half the money, every month
Roll the 26 filing names up to parents (Step 2) and the monthly cadence gets simple. Without the rollup this chart is a lie, AbbVie’s lead shrinks and Johnson & Johnson splits into five entities that all look mid-pack.


Monthly payment volume by parent company
top4 <- payments %>%
  filter(!is_outlier) %>%
  count(parent, wt = amount_usd, name = "spend") %>%
  slice_max(spend, n = 4) %>%
  pull(parent)

monthly_trend <- payments %>%
  filter(!is_outlier, parent %in% top4) %>%
  group_by(year_month, parent) %>%
  summarise(total = sum(amount_usd) / 1e6, .groups = "drop")

trend_palette <- setNames(c(NAVY, BLUE, GREEN, AMBER), top4)

## abbvie's share of ALL dollars, the headline reads this
abbvie_share <- payments %>%
  summarise(s = sum(amount_usd[parent == top4[1]]) / sum(amount_usd) * 100) %>%
  pull(s)

## company names ride the end of their own lines. the bottom two finish the
## year close together so they each get a nudge, otherwise the labels collide
ends <- monthly_trend %>%
  group_by(parent) %>%
  slice_max(year_month, n = 1) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  arrange(total) %>%
  mutate(label_y = total + c(-0.55, 0.55, 0, 0))

ggplot(monthly_trend, aes(year_month, total, color = parent)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_point(size = 2) +
  geom_text(data = ends, aes(y = label_y, label = parent),
            hjust = 0, nudge_x = 9, size = 3.5, fontface = "bold") +
  scale_color_manual(values = trend_palette) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = label_dollar(suffix = "M")) +
  ## explicit breaks, otherwise the room reserved for the line end labels
  ## grows phantom month ticks past december
  scale_x_date(breaks = seq(min(monthly_trend$year_month),
                            max(monthly_trend$year_month), by = "month"),
               date_labels = "%b",
               expand = expansion(mult = c(0.02, 0.24))) +
  labs(
    title = sprintf("%s alone is %.0f%% of all the %d money", top4[1], abbvie_share, summary_year),
    subtitle = "monthly payments by parent company, top 4 of 7, flagged outliers excluded",
    x = NULL, y = NULL
  ) +
  theme_exec() +
  theme(panel.grid.major.x = element_blank())





Monthly payments by parent company, top 4 of 7 shown, flagged outliers excluded. Names label the lines directly, no legend.

title: Automated Reporting Pipeline
section: Step 4: Render to Excel via openxlsx2
text:
Step 4: Render to Excel via openxlsx2
Some stakeholders need formatted Excel and pretending otherwise just costs you credibility. Generate it from the same cleaned data, alongside the HTML, and everyone gets the format they actually open.


Generate formatted Excel workbook
library(openxlsx2)

## three sheets: the summary, the line level detail, and the flagged
## outliers on their own sheet so nothing silently disappears
wb <- wb_workbook(creator = "Analytics Pipeline") %>%
  wb_add_worksheet("Summary", grid_lines = FALSE) %>%
  wb_add_data(x = company_summary, start_row = 3, start_col = 2) %>%
  wb_add_cell_style(dims = "B3:G3", horizontal = "center", text_bold = TRUE) %>%
  wb_freeze_pane(first_active_row = 4) %>%

  wb_add_worksheet("Detail", grid_lines = FALSE) %>%
  wb_add_data(
    x = payments %>% filter(parent == target_company, !is_outlier) %>%
      select(payment_date, payment_type_short, specialty, state, amount_usd),
    start_row = 1
  ) %>%
  wb_freeze_pane(first_active_row = 2) %>%

  wb_add_worksheet("Outliers Flagged", grid_lines = FALSE) %>%
  wb_add_data(
    x = payments %>% filter(parent == target_company, is_outlier),
    start_row = 1
  )

### in the real pipeline:
### wb_save(wb, paste0("output/", target_company, "_2024.xlsx"))
cat("in production: saved to output/", target_company, "_2024.xlsx\n", sep = "")
cat("workbook structure: 3 sheets (Summary, Detail, Outliers Flagged)\n")

title: Automated Reporting Pipeline
text:
Step 5: The runner script
In production all of the above lives in functions called from a parameterized runner. One command, full deliverable, any company:
# what the team actually runs each month
quarto render reports/monthly-summary.qmd \
  -P company:"AbbVie" \
  -P period:"2024" \
  --output-dir output/abbvie-2024/
Or loop the whole roster in one batch:
## render reports for every parent company in one shot
companies_to_render <- unique(payments$parent)

purrr::walk(companies_to_render, function(co) {
  quarto::quarto_render(
    "reports/monthly-summary.qmd",
    execute_params = list(company = co, period = "2024"),
    output_file = paste0("output/", janitor::make_clean_names(co), "_2024.html")
  )
})

title: Automated Reporting Pipeline
text:
What this demonstrates
R fluency where R is the right answer. Quarto + gt + ggplot2 + openxlsx2 is a genuinely first class reporting stack, and this shows the pieces working together the way they would in production.
Entity resolution is everywhere. 26 raw payer names collapse to 7 parent companies, and skipping that step changes the answer to every company level question on this page. The rollup has rough edges worth admitting, the regex map is maintained by hand, so the day CMS adds a new subsidiary it lands unmapped until someone notices. The unmapped names keep their raw form on purpose so they show up instead of vanishing into an Other bucket. Production wants a reviewed mapping table, not a smarter regex.
Flag, don’t delete. Outliers get flagged inside each payment type and carried through to their own Excel sheet. A $22,000 royalty is normal and a $22,000 meal is not, a global threshold can’t tell those apart.
The methodology paper, made concrete. Everything here is the practical implementation of The Case for Code-Driven Reporting, the same discipline applied to a real recurring deliverable.

Orchestration with targets for dependency tracking and incremental rebuilds
Tests for the cleaning functions with testthat, which would catch upstream schema changes the day they happen, and a unit test on the company rollup map
renv lockfile so the whole team renders against identical dependencies
CI/CD, auto render on data refresh via GitHub Actions
Email and Slack delivery, finished reports distributed without anyone asking

title: Detecting Media-Driven Spikes in Wikipedia Traffic
text: Spike Detection · Real Public Data

title: Detecting Media-Driven Spikes in Wikipedia Traffic
text:
The Business Problem
In media measurement you face the same question constantly: did a traffic spike actually mean something, or is it noise? A 30% jump in sessions might be a successful TV spot, a viral social moment, or just a normal Saturday.
Answering that manually doesn’t scale. With daily data across many pages, products, or regions, you need an automated framework that:

Builds a dynamic baseline per series instead of one global threshold
Flags spikes that are statistically unusual, not just big in absolute terms
Handles both flavors of spike: the one-day blip and the multi-day slow burn
Ranks what it finds, because an alert list nobody can triage is the same as no alert list

Wikipedia pageviews are a perfect testbed. The data is real, it’s public, and the summer of 2024 (Olympics, an assassination attempt, a presidential race) is packed with genuine media-driven spikes to find.

title: Detecting Media-Driven Spikes in Wikipedia Traffic
text:
The Approach



1


Ingest real data to Parquet
Pull daily pageviews from the Wikimedia REST API for 103 articles over 92 days, write to month-partitioned Parquet, query lazily with Polars.




2


Plant a known answer key
Inject four labeled spikes with chosen multipliers and durations (×2 to ×5, 1 to 4 days) so detection quality can be measured, not eyeballed.




3


Build robust baselines
Rolling 21-day median plus MAD-scaled sigma per article. Median and MAD don’t get corrupted by the very outliers we’re hunting.




4


Detect in two tiers, then rank
A persistent tier (z > 5 for 2+ consecutive days) and an extreme tier (z > 12 for even a single day). Group flagged days into events and rank by peak z for triage.

title: Detecting Media-Driven Spikes in Wikipedia Traffic
text:
Step 1: Real data, real ingestion
Wikipedia publishes per-article pageview counts through the Pageviews REST API. The per-article endpoint is daily grain (the hourly variant was retired; hourly still exists as multi-GB bulk dumps at ~7M rows per hour, which is where this exact pipeline would point in production). Each row is one (article, day) pair: article, timestamp, views.
The fetch script already cached everything as partitioned Parquet, so loading is a lazy Polars scan. I time it because the query speed is part of the point.


Load real Wikipedia pageviews from the Parquet cache
import json
import time
from pathlib import Path
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import polars as pl

CACHE = Path("../data/cache/wikipedia")
if not CACHE.exists() or not any(CACHE.glob("year_month=*/*.parquet")):
    raise FileNotFoundError(
        "Wikipedia pageview cache not found. Run "
        "`python data/fetch_wikipedia.py` from the project root first."
    )

t0 = time.perf_counter()
wiki = (
    pl.scan_parquet(str(CACHE / "**/*.parquet"))
    .filter(pl.col("project") == "en.wikipedia")
    .select(["article", "timestamp", "views"])
    .sort(["article", "timestamp"])
    .collect()
)
query_ms = (time.perf_counter() - t0) * 1000

df = wiki.to_pandas()
df["timestamp"] = pd.to_datetime(df["timestamp"])

n_articles = df["article"].nunique()
n_days = df["timestamp"].nunique()
print(f"loaded {len(df):,} rows in {query_ms:.0f} ms (lazy scan + predicate pushdown)")
print(f"  {n_articles} articles x {n_days} days, {df['timestamp'].min().date()} to {df['timestamp'].max().date()}")
print(f"\nsample rows:")
print(df.head(5).to_string(index=False))


loaded 9,476 rows in 3 ms (lazy scan + predicate pushdown)
  103 articles x 92 days, 2024-07-01 to 2024-09-30

sample rows:
        article  timestamp  views
Abraham_Lincoln 2024-07-01  13790
Abraham_Lincoln 2024-07-02  13548
Abraham_Lincoln 2024-07-03  15240
Abraham_Lincoln 2024-07-04  19220
Abraham_Lincoln 2024-07-05  15605

title: Detecting Media-Driven Spikes in Wikipedia Traffic
text:
Step 2: The partitioned Parquet warehouse
The engineering payoff is in the storage layer. The fetch script writes Hive-style month-partitioned Parquet (year_month=YYYY-MM/), the same pattern the Data Foundation page describes, just at a coarser grain than production hourly data would use.






Note



On partition grain. I deliberately partition by month, not by day, even though the data is daily. With 103 articles × 92 days, a per-day partition would mean ~100 rows per file, small enough that Parquet’s per-file metadata overhead dominates the actual column data and the format loses to plain CSV. Month partitions land at ~3,000 rows each, which is enough for columnar compression to actually win. The same dataset at hourly bulk-dump grain (~7M rows/hour) would partition by date with ease. Pick the partition column to match the grain of the data, not the calendar.




Quantify the Parquet warehouse vs CSV equivalent
import os

total_size = sum(
    os.path.getsize(os.path.join(dp, f))
    for dp, _, files in os.walk(CACHE)
    for f in files
    if f.endswith('.parquet')
)
n_partitions = len([p for p in CACHE.iterdir() if p.is_dir()])

## write the row-equivalent CSV purely for the size comparison
csv_path = '/tmp/wiki_full.csv'
df.to_csv(csv_path, index=False)
csv_size = os.path.getsize(csv_path)

### key results reads this at render time, no hand-typed numbers
compression_ratio = csv_size / max(total_size, 1)

print(f"Parquet warehouse: {CACHE}")
print(f"  partitions:      {n_partitions} (one per year-month)")
print(f"  total size:      {total_size / 1024**2:.2f} MB")
print(f"  equivalent CSV:  {csv_size / 1024**2:.2f} MB")
print(f"  compression:     {compression_ratio:.1f}x smaller")


Parquet warehouse: ../data/cache/wikipedia
  partitions:      3 (one per year-month)
  total size:      0.06 MB
  equivalent CSV:  0.27 MB
  compression:     4.3x smaller

title: Detecting Media-Driven Spikes in Wikipedia Traffic
text:
Step 3: Plant the answer key
Here’s the part most anomaly detection writeups skip. If I only run the detector on raw data and point at dramatic charts, you have to take my word that the flags are good. Real spikes don’t arrive labeled. So before detection, I plant four fake spikes with exact known dates, sizes, and durations on top of the real data. That gives the validation step an answer key: we know precisely what a perfect detector should find, because we put it there.
The four are chosen to stress different failure modes, not picked at random:










Article
Planted scenario
Multiplier
Duration
What it tests




Albert_Einstein
“Breaking News”
×4
2 days starting Jul 21
a sharp burst on a steady page


Taylor_Swift
“Celebrity Mention”
×3
3 days starting Aug 5
a spike on a page that is already busy and noisy


Bitcoin
“Viral Social”
×2
4 days starting Aug 25
the smallest lift but the longest, a slow burn


Elon_Musk
“TV Mention”
×5
1 day on Sep 9
a one-day blip, the case a persistence rule misses



The scenario names are just labels I made up for the shape of each event, they aren’t real news stories. ×N means every real pageview count inside that window gets multiplied by N, so the spike sits on top of the page’s genuine day-to-day texture instead of being a clean synthetic curve.
The sizes are deliberately modest. In my experience real media-driven lifts land somewhere between 2× and 5× of normal activity, a true 8× or 10× day is rare. An earlier draft of this study planted ×8 and ×12 spikes and they were almost insulting to the detector, anything would catch those. The ×2 Bitcoin slow burn is the one that actually makes the detector work for it.


Inject the four labeled spike events into the real data
spike_events = json.loads((CACHE / "_spike_events.json").read_text())

## plant each spike and show exactly what changed, before and after,
## so there's no mystery about what the detector is being graded on
print("planting the answer key:")
for evt in spike_events:
    start = pd.Timestamp(evt["start"])
    end = start + pd.Timedelta(days=evt["duration_days"])
    mask = (
        (df["article"] == evt["article"])
        & (df["timestamp"] >= start)
        & (df["timestamp"] < end)
    )
    before = int(df.loc[mask, "views"].mean())
    df.loc[mask, "views"] = (df.loc[mask, "views"] * evt["multiplier"]).astype(int)
    after = int(df.loc[mask, "views"].mean())
    print(f"  {evt['article']:<16} x{evt['multiplier']:>4.0f} for {evt['duration_days']}d "
          f"from {start.date()}  ({before:>7,} avg views/day -> {after:>8,})")

total_planted_days = sum(e['duration_days'] for e in spike_events)
print(f"\n{total_planted_days} planted days out of {len(df):,} total rows,"
      f" everything else is raw Wikipedia traffic")


planting the answer key:
  Albert_Einstein  x   8 for 2d from 2024-07-21  ( 13,118 avg views/day ->  104,944)
  Taylor_Swift     x   5 for 3d from 2024-08-05  ( 29,271 avg views/day ->  146,356)
  Bitcoin          x   4 for 4d from 2024-08-25  (  6,229 avg views/day ->   24,918)
  Elon_Musk        x  12 for 1d from 2024-09-09  ( 39,879 avg views/day ->  478,548)

10 planted days out of 9,476 total rows, everything else is raw Wikipedia traffic

title: Detecting Media-Driven Spikes in Wikipedia Traffic
text:
Step 4: The detection algorithm
Robust statistics matter here. Mean and standard deviation break down when outliers are the thing you’re trying to detect. The outlier inflates the standard deviation, which raises the threshold, which masks the next outlier. A classic feedback loop (there’s a chart of this failure further down).
The fix is median + MAD (median absolute deviation). The median ignores extreme values, and MAD scaled by 1.4826 is a consistent estimator of the standard deviation under normality, so the z-scores stay interpretable.
Detection runs in two tiers, because real spikes come in two shapes:

Persistent tier: robust z > 5 for at least 2 consecutive days. Catches sustained events while filtering one-day noise. This is the tier that earns the ×2 Bitcoin slow burn.
Extreme tier: robust z > 12 on any single day. Exists because a genuinely large one-day event (our planted ×5 Elon Musk day lands around z ≈ 47) should not be thrown away just because it didn’t persist.



Robust tiered spike detection: rolling median + MAD
### tuned on this dataset: z>5 alone flagged too much (daily wiki traffic is
### heavy tailed), z>5 with 2-day persistence missed the 1-day planted spike.
### two tiers handle both shapes.
WINDOW_DAYS = 21      ## 3 weeks of trailing history for the baseline
Z_PERSISTENT = 5.0    ## threshold for the persistent tier
Z_EXTREME = 12.0      ## a single day this far out counts on its own
MIN_PERSIST = 2       ## consecutive days required in the persistent tier

def detect_spikes_for_article(grp):
    """robust two-tier spike detection for one article's daily series"""
    grp = grp.sort_values('timestamp').reset_index(drop=True)

    ## rolling robust baseline
    grp['rolling_median'] = grp['views'].rolling(WINDOW_DAYS, min_periods=7).median()
    grp['rolling_mad'] = (
        grp['views'].rolling(WINDOW_DAYS, min_periods=7)
        .apply(lambda x: np.median(np.abs(x - np.median(x))), raw=True)
    )
    ### scale MAD so it's comparable to a std dev under normality
    grp['rolling_sigma'] = grp['rolling_mad'] * 1.4826

    ## z-score against the robust baseline
    grp['robust_z'] = (
        (grp['views'] - grp['rolling_median'])
        / grp['rolling_sigma'].replace(0, np.nan)
    )

    ## tier 1: persistent. find runs of consecutive days above Z_PERSISTENT
    ## and keep whole runs that last at least MIN_PERSIST days
    grp['above'] = grp['robust_z'] > Z_PERSISTENT
    run_id = (~grp['above']).cumsum()
    run_len = grp.groupby(run_id)['above'].transform('sum')
    grp['persistent'] = grp['above'] & (run_len >= MIN_PERSIST)

    ## tier 2: extreme. one day this far above baseline stands on its own
    grp['extreme'] = grp['robust_z'] > Z_EXTREME

    grp['is_spike'] = grp['persistent'] | grp['extreme']
    return grp

## manual loop instead of groupby().apply(), reads cleaner and dodges the
## include_groups deprecation dance in pandas 2.2+
per_article = []
for article_name, grp in df.groupby('article'):
    res = detect_spikes_for_article(grp.copy())
    res['article'] = article_name
    per_article.append(res)
results = pd.concat(per_article, ignore_index=True)

## group consecutive flagged days into distinct events, then rank.
## an alert list is only useful if it's ranked
flagged = results[results['is_spike']].copy()
flagged['gap'] = flagged.groupby('article')['timestamp'].diff().dt.days.fillna(99)
flagged['event_id'] = (flagged['gap'] > 1).groupby(flagged['article']).cumsum()

events = (
    flagged.groupby(['article', 'event_id'])
    .agg(
        start=('timestamp', 'min'),
        n_days=('is_spike', 'sum'),
        peak_z=('robust_z', 'max'),
        peak_views=('views', 'max'),
        baseline=('rolling_median', 'median'),
    )
    .reset_index()
    .sort_values('peak_z', ascending=False)
    .reset_index(drop=True)
)
events['lift'] = (events['peak_views'] / events['baseline']).round(1)

## peak date per event, the charts use this
peak_dates = []
for _, r in events.iterrows():
    sub = flagged[(flagged['article'] == r['article']) & (flagged['event_id'] == r['event_id'])]
    peak_dates.append(sub.loc[sub['robust_z'].idxmax(), 'timestamp'])
events['peak_date'] = peak_dates

n_events = len(events)
n_flagged_articles = events['article'].nunique()
print(f"{n_events} distinct events across {n_flagged_articles} of {n_articles} articles")
print(f"\ntop 12 events by peak robust z:")
show = events.head(12).copy()
show['start'] = show['start'].dt.date
show['peak_z'] = show['peak_z'].round(1)
print(show[['article', 'start', 'n_days', 'peak_z', 'lift']].to_string(index=False))


92 distinct events across 59 of 103 articles

top 12 events by peak robust z:
           article      start  n_days  peak_z  lift
        Jeff_Bezos 2024-07-23       3   163.1  11.6
         Lady_Gaga 2024-07-26       5   145.5  20.9
         Elon_Musk 2024-09-09       1   127.6  12.7
   Albert_Einstein 2024-07-21       2   121.8   8.0
      Taylor_Swift 2024-08-05       4    81.9   5.8
      Isaac_Newton 2024-07-24       2    79.8   6.3
      Eiffel_Tower 2024-07-25       8    75.2   7.5
Theodore_Roosevelt 2024-07-13       4    60.5   9.1
    Warren_Buffett 2024-08-03       7    57.1   4.2
  Ernest_Hemingway 2024-07-21       2    51.6  11.3
       Tiger_Woods 2024-07-16       8    46.8   5.6
   John_F._Kennedy 2024-07-13       5    45.7   6.8


That top 12 is worth a pause. Two of the planted spikes make the list, but the gentler two (×3 Taylor Swift, ×2 Bitcoin) rank further down, below real events like Lady Gaga and the Eiffel Tower (Paris Olympics opening ceremony, July 26) and Theodore Roosevelt and JFK starting July 13, the day of the Trump rally shooting, when readers went looking up past assassination attempts. That’s the correct behavior: the real news of summer 2024 genuinely out-spiked my modest planted lifts, and the ranking says so. The detector doesn’t know any of the news, it just knows which days sat far outside each page’s baseline.

title: Detecting Media-Driven Spikes in Wikipedia Traffic
section: Step 5: Validate against the answer key
text:
Step 5: Validate against the answer key
Now the graded part. For each planted event, did the detector flag the actual planted days, and which tier caught it?


Grade the detector against the planted ground truth
print("answer key vs detector, day by day:")
print("=" * 78)

n_caught = 0
days_caught = 0
days_planted = 0
for evt in spike_events:
    s = pd.Timestamp(evt['start'])
    e = s + pd.Timedelta(days=evt['duration_days'])
    w = results[
        (results['article'] == evt['article'])
        & (results['timestamp'] >= s)
        & (results['timestamp'] < e)
    ]
    hit = int(w['is_spike'].sum())
    days_caught += hit
    days_planted += len(w)
    tiers = []
    if w['persistent'].any():
        tiers.append('persistent')
    if w['extreme'].any():
        tiers.append('extreme')
    if hit == len(w):
        n_caught += 1
        verdict = "CAUGHT"
    elif hit > 0:
        verdict = "PARTIAL"
    else:
        verdict = "MISSED"
    print(f"  {evt['article']:<16} x{evt['multiplier']:>4.0f} {evt['duration_days']}d   "
          f"{hit}/{len(w)} planted days flagged   "
          f"peak z={w['robust_z'].max():5.1f}   {verdict} ({' + '.join(tiers)})")

recall = n_caught / len(spike_events)
print(f"\nrecall on planted events: {n_caught}/{len(spike_events)} ({recall:.0%})")
print(f"planted days flagged:     {days_caught}/{days_planted}")

## the tier design earns its keep right here: the 1-day x12 spike has no
## persistence to offer, only the extreme tier saves it
musk = results[
    (results['article'] == 'Elon_Musk')
    & (results['timestamp'] == pd.Timestamp('2024-09-09'))
].iloc[0]
print(f"\nthe one-day Elon_Musk spike: persistent tier = {bool(musk['persistent'])}, "
      f"extreme tier = {bool(musk['extreme'])} (z={musk['robust_z']:.0f})")
print("a persistence-only detector throws this event away entirely")


answer key vs detector, day by day:
==============================================================================
  Albert_Einstein  x   8 2d   2/2 planted days flagged   peak z=121.8   CAUGHT (persistent + extreme)
  Taylor_Swift     x   5 3d   3/3 planted days flagged   peak z= 81.9   CAUGHT (persistent + extreme)
  Bitcoin          x   4 4d   4/4 planted days flagged   peak z= 23.6   CAUGHT (persistent + extreme)
  Elon_Musk        x  12 1d   1/1 planted days flagged   peak z=127.6   CAUGHT (extreme)

recall on planted events: 4/4 (100%)
planted days flagged:     10/10

the one-day Elon_Musk spike: persistent tier = False, extreme tier = True (z=128)
a persistence-only detector throws this event away entirely


One honest note on precision: I don’t report a false positive rate against the planted events, because the other flags aren’t false. This window of real data contains the Olympics, a rally shooting, a party convention, and the home stretch of an election. Lots of pages genuinely spiked. The planted events answer “does the detector catch a spike of known size,” and the ranked event list answers “is the output triageable.”

section: The detector at work, on all four planted spikes
text:
The detector at work, on all four planted spikes


Shared chart style and helpers
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import matplotlib.dates as mdates

## same palette as the rest of the portfolio. blue for data, slate for
## baselines, red strictly means detected / planted
NAVY, BLUE, SLATE, RED, GRAY = '#0f172a', '#2563eb', '#94a3b8', '#dc2626', '#64748b'

plt.rcParams.update({
    'axes.spines.top': False,
    'axes.spines.right': False,
    'figure.facecolor': 'white',
    'font.size': 10,
})

# def headline(ax, title, sub):
#     ### takeaway big and bold, literal description small and gray
#     ax.set_title(title, fontsize=14, fontweight='bold', loc='left', pad=30, color=NAVY)
#     ax.text(0, 1.06, sub, transform=ax.transAxes, fontsize=9.5, color=GRAY)
def headline(ax, title, subtitle):
    ax.set_title(title, fontsize=14, fontweight='bold', loc='left', pad=30, color=NAVY)
    ax.text(0.0, 1.02, subtitle.strip(),
        transform=ax.transAxes,fontsize=9.5, color=GRAY, ha='left', va='bottom')


Each panel below is one planted spike. The shaded band is the planted window (the answer key), the dots are what the detector flagged. The grade is visual: dots inside every band.


Planted windows vs detected days, one panel per event
# fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 2, figsize=(13, 8.5))
fig, axes = plt.subplots(4, 1, figsize=(6.5, 13))
axes = axes.flatten()

for ax, evt in zip(axes, spike_events):
    s = pd.Timestamp(evt['start'])
    e = s + pd.Timedelta(days=evt['duration_days'])
    art = results[results['article'] == evt['article']]

    ## the planted window first so everything draws on top of it
    ax.axvspan(s, e, color=RED, alpha=0.08, zorder=0)

    ## the normal range band. days outside this gray zone are what the
    ## detector considers outlier territory
    ax.fill_between(
        art['timestamp'],
        art['rolling_median'] - 3 * art['rolling_sigma'],
        art['rolling_median'] + 3 * art['rolling_sigma'],
        color=SLATE, alpha=0.18, zorder=1,
    )

    ax.plot(art['timestamp'], art['views'], color=BLUE, linewidth=1.1, zorder=2)
    ax.plot(art['timestamp'], art['rolling_median'], color=SLATE, linewidth=1.4, zorder=2)

    hits = art[art['is_spike']]
    ax.scatter(hits['timestamp'], hits['views'], color=RED, s=26, zorder=5)

    ## one annotation per panel: what we planted, anchored to the peak
    ## inside the planted window (the page max can be a real event, taylor's
    ## september endorsement spike actually tops her planted one)
    win = art[(art['timestamp'] >= s) & (art['timestamp'] < e)]
    peak = win.loc[win['views'].idxmax()]
    ax.annotate(
        f"planted x{evt['multiplier']:.0f}, {evt['duration_days']}d",
        xy=(peak['timestamp'], peak['views']),
        xytext=(12, -4), textcoords='offset points',
        fontsize=9.5, fontweight='bold', color=RED,
    )

    ax.set_title(evt['article'].replace('_', ' '), fontsize=11.5,
                 fontweight='bold', loc='left', color=NAVY)
    ax.set_ylabel('daily views', fontsize=9)
    ax.tick_params(labelsize=8)
    ax.xaxis.set_major_formatter(mdates.DateFormatter('%b %d'))
    ax.margins(y=0.1)

plt.suptitle('All four planted spikes caught, dots land inside every red window',
             fontsize=12.5, fontweight='bold', x=0.005, ha='left', color=NAVY)
# plt.tight_layout(rect=[0, 0, 0, 1])
plt.tight_layout(rect=[0, 0, 1, 0.96], h_pad=2.0)

plt.show()





The four planted spikes. Blue line: daily views (real data plus the planted multiplier). Gray band: the page’s normal range, rolling median ±3 robust σ, anything outside it is an outlier candidate. Red band: the planted window. Red dots: days the detector flagged.

section: Validating the spikes I didn’t plant
text:
Validating the spikes I didn’t plant
The Taylor Swift and Elon Musk panels show red dots outside the planted windows, so either the detector is throwing false alarms on these pages or those are real events. We can check, because every flag comes with a date and the news record for summer 2024 is easy to audit.


List every non-planted flagged day on the four planted pages
print("flagged days on the planted pages, outside the planted windows:")
print("=" * 70)
for evt in spike_events:
    s = pd.Timestamp(evt['start'])
    e = s + pd.Timedelta(days=evt['duration_days'])
    extra = flagged[
        (flagged['article'] == evt['article'])
        & ~((flagged['timestamp'] >= s) & (flagged['timestamp'] < e))
    ]
    label = f"{len(extra)} day(s)" if len(extra) else "none, page was quiet"
    print(f"\n  {evt['article']} ({label}):")
    for _, r in extra.iterrows():
        print(f"    {r['timestamp'].date()}   {r['views']:>9,} views"
              f"   vs baseline {r['rolling_median']:>9,.0f}   z={r['robust_z']:.1f}")


flagged days on the planted pages, outside the planted windows:
======================================================================

  Albert_Einstein (none, page was quiet):

  Taylor_Swift (4 day(s)):
    2024-08-08      59,851 views   vs baseline    30,358   z=15.7
    2024-09-11     114,520 views   vs baseline    29,258   z=16.0
    2024-09-12     105,249 views   vs baseline    29,258   z=14.3
    2024-09-13      59,996 views   vs baseline    29,258   z=5.8

  Bitcoin (none, page was quiet):

  Elon_Musk (4 day(s)):
    2024-07-13      38,565 views   vs baseline    28,254   z=8.2
    2024-07-14      44,844 views   vs baseline    28,479   z=10.6
    2024-07-16      48,812 views   vs baseline    28,752   z=9.7
    2024-07-17      62,676 views   vs baseline    28,800   z=16.2


Every one of those checks out against the news record, none of them are false alarms:

Taylor Swift, Aug 8 — her three Vienna Eras Tour shows were cancelled on Aug 7 after authorities foiled a planned attack on the concerts. Huge global story.
Taylor Swift, Sep 11 to 13 — she endorsed Kamala Harris on Instagram the night of the Sep 10 presidential debate. Her page roughly quadrupled its baseline for two days.
Elon Musk, Jul 13 to 17 — the rally shooting happened Jul 13 and Musk endorsed Trump within hours, then spent the week in the news cycle (reports of a major pro-Trump PAC pledge followed). Four flagged days riding one news wave.

Einstein and Bitcoin show nothing outside their planted windows, which is its own kind of validation: the detector stays quiet on pages where nothing happened. So on these four pages the complete flag list is four planted spikes plus three real news stories, and zero unexplained alarms.

title: Detecting Media-Driven Spikes in Wikipedia Traffic
section: Why median + MAD instead of mean + std
text:
Why median + MAD instead of mean + std
This is the methodological core of the piece, so here’s the failure made visible. Same data (Taylor Swift with the planted ×3 spike), two detectors. The classic mean + 3·std band swallows its own spike: the outlier days inflate the rolling std so much that the threshold balloons above the data for the next three weeks. The robust band barely moves.


Classic mean+std threshold vs robust median+MAD on the same spike
ts = results[results['article'] == 'Taylor_Swift'].sort_values('timestamp').copy()

## classic detector on the identical window settings
ts['c_mean'] = ts['views'].rolling(WINDOW_DAYS, min_periods=7).mean()
ts['c_std'] = ts['views'].rolling(WINDOW_DAYS, min_periods=7).std()
ts['classic_upper'] = ts['c_mean'] + 3 * ts['c_std']
ts['robust_upper'] = ts['rolling_median'] + 3 * ts['rolling_sigma']

evt = [e for e in spike_events if e['article'] == 'Taylor_Swift'][0]
s = pd.Timestamp(evt['start'])
e = s + pd.Timedelta(days=evt['duration_days'])

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(9, 5))

ax.axvspan(s, e, color=RED, alpha=0.08, zorder=0)
ax.plot(ts['timestamp'], ts['views'], color=BLUE, linewidth=1.2, label='daily views')
ax.plot(ts['timestamp'], ts['robust_upper'], color=NAVY, linewidth=2,label='robust threshold (median + 3·MADσ)')
ax.plot(ts['timestamp'], ts['classic_upper'], color=GRAY, linewidth=1.3,linestyle='--', label='classic threshold (mean + 3·std)')

## point at the failure, that's the whole chart
post = ts[(ts['timestamp'] > e) & (ts['timestamp'] <= e + pd.Timedelta(days=18))]
worst = post.loc[post['classic_upper'].idxmax()]
ax.annotate('the spike inflates the classic\nthreshold for 3 weeks after',
            xy=(worst['timestamp'], worst['classic_upper']),
            xytext=(25, -70), textcoords='offset points',
            fontsize=9.5, fontweight='bold', color=GRAY,
            arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle='->', color=GRAY, lw=1.2,
                            connectionstyle='arc3,rad=0.25'))
ymin, ymax = ax.get_ylim()
ax.text(s, ymin + 0.04 * (ymax - ymin), f" planted ×{evt['multiplier']:.0f} window",
        fontsize=9, color=RED, fontweight='bold')

ax.set_ylabel('daily views')
ax.xaxis.set_major_formatter(mdates.DateFormatter('%b %d'))
ax.legend(fontsize=9, loc='upper left', frameon=False)

headline(ax, 'Outliers raise a classic detector\'s own threshold',
         'same series, same window, two thresholds. robust statistics don\'t flinch')

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()





Taylor Swift daily views with the planted ×3 spike. The classic threshold (dashed) inflates after contact with a spike and stays blind for a full window length, you can see it happen again after her real September spike. The robust threshold (solid navy) is barely disturbed.

## context bands: the olympics, and july 13. labels pinned just inside the

oly_s, oly_e = pd.Timestamp('2024-07-26'), pd.Timestamp('2024-08-11')

ax.text(j13, 0.88, 'Jul 13 rally shooting ', ha='right', transform=trans,
        fontsize=9.5, color=GRAY, fontweight='bold')

## label the planted four, custom offsets so einstein and gaga don't collide
planted_offsets = {
    'Albert_Einstein': (3, -17, 'left'),
    'Taylor_Swift': (8, 2, 'left'),
    'Bitcoin': (8, -3, 'left'),
    'Elon_Musk': (8, 2, 'left'),

for _, r in pla.iterrows():

    ax.annotate(r['article'].replace('_', ' '), xy=(r['peak_date'], r['peak_z']),
                xytext=(dx, dy), textcoords='offset points', ha=ha,
                fontsize=9, fontweight='bold', color=RED)

## plus the real events i can actually name
callouts = {
    'Lady_Gaga': ('sang at the opening ceremony', (8, -12)),
    'Theodore_Roosevelt': ('readers compare past assassination attempts', (-2, 12)),
    'Barack_Obama': ('DNC speech', (8, -1)),
}
for art, (why, (dx, dy)) in callouts.items():

    ax.annotate(f"{art.replace('_', ' ')}\n{why}", xy=(r['peak_date'], r['peak_z']),
                xytext=(dx, dy), textcoords='offset points',

ax.set_yscale('log')
ax.set_ylabel('peak robust z (log scale)')
ax.xaxis.set_major_formatter(mdates.DateFormatter('%b %d'))
ax.grid(axis='y', linestyle=':', alpha=0.4)

headline(ax, 'The detector reconstructs the news cycle of summer 2024',
         f'\n{n_events} detected events across {n_flagged_articles} articles. red diamonds are the planted answer key, blue dots are real')

# plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()





All detected events, peak robust z by peak date (log scale). Red: the four planted spikes. Blue: real events in the raw data. The Olympics band and the July 13 news cycle need no help from me.




The clusters are real news, found with zero knowledge of the news. Theodore Roosevelt, JFK, Lincoln, and FDR all spike within a day of July 13 (readers looking up past assassination attempts). The Eiffel Tower, Lady Gaga, and a dozen others light up when the Olympics open on July 26. Obama spikes the morning after his August 20 DNC speech. In a production media-monitoring setting this chart is the product: a ranked, dated feed of “something happened to this page” with the sizes attached.

Key Results

4.3× Storage compression Parquet vs CSV, measured at render

title: Detecting Media-Driven Spikes in Wikipedia Traffic
text:
What this demonstrates
The methodology: spike detection with median + MAD instead of mean + std, because the anomalies you’re hunting will corrupt classical statistics (the comparison chart above shows exactly how). Two detection tiers because one-day blips and slow burns are both real and one rule can’t serve both.
The validation: I planted four spikes with known sizes and dates and graded the detector against them, instead of pointing at charts and declaring victory. The grading is day-level, not “did the article show up somewhere in the flag list.”
The honesty: the thresholds were tuned on this dataset and I say so. The planted events measure recall, not precision, and I say that too. The real events the detector surfaced (Olympics, July 13, the DNC) are the strongest evidence it works, precisely because I didn’t plant them.

title: Detecting Media-Driven Spikes in Wikipedia Traffic
section: What I’d extend in a production version
text:
What I’d extend in a production version

CUSUM or Bayesian online changepoint detection for slow level shifts, the MAD approach is built for sharp spikes
Per-series threshold learning — one global z threshold across 103 very different pages is the biggest simplification here; volatility profiles differ by page
Cause attribution — join detected events against news APIs, social trends, or a media schedule so events arrive pre-explained instead of pre-ranked

title: Political Media Spend Patterns in FEC Data
text: Media Spend Analysis · Real Public Data

title: Political Media Spend Patterns in FEC Data
text:
The Business Problem
Political ad spend is one of the few areas where real, granular media buying data is publicly available. Federal political committees must disclose every disbursement, including ad buys, to the FEC. The bulk data files contain millions of records spanning decades.
For a media measurement professional, this is gold, but the format is gnarly and very messy. Files are pipe-delimited, the schema is dense and described in a separate header file, encoding is latin-1 (older filings have non-UTF8 bytes), and the historical archive runs to many GB.
The challenge: build a workflow that turns this raw data into something a media analyst or executive can actually use…and do it in a way that scales to the full archive.

title: Political Media Spend Patterns in FEC Data
text:
The Approach



1


Parse the FEC format
Pull the pipe-delimited bulk files from fec.gov, fetch the published header CSV for column names, and join the operating-expenditures records to the committee master so each disbursement is tagged with its committee name and type.




2


Design a partition strategy
Partition by election cycle (high-cardinality, queries naturally cycle-bounded) and month within cycle (mid-cardinality, supports time-series queries).




3


Categorize disbursements
Map free-text purpose descriptions to standardized spend categories (TV, digital, direct mail, etc.) using a deterministic rule-based classifier.




4


Surface analytical patterns
Aggregate to channel × cycle × committee-type level and produce executive-ready visualizations of spend allocation patterns.

title: Political Media Spend Patterns in FEC Data
section: Step 1: Load the FEC parquet cache
text:
Step 1: Load the FEC parquet cache
The fetch script (data/fetch_fec.py) downloads the operating expenditures and committee master zips from fec.gov, joins them on committee ID, and writes the result to cycle/month-partitioned Parquet. We read from there.


Load real FEC operating expenditures from Parquet cache
from pathlib import Path
import numpy as np
import polars as pl
import pandas as pd

CACHE = Path("../data/cache/fec")
if not CACHE.exists() or not any(CACHE.rglob("*.parquet")):
    raise FileNotFoundError(
        "FEC cache not found. Run `python data/fetch_fec.py` from the project "
        "root first (downloads ~1 GB across the 2020/2022/2024 cycles)."
    )

oppexp_raw = (
    pl.scan_parquet(str(CACHE / "**/*.parquet"), hive_partitioning=True)
    .select([
        "cmte_id", "cmte_nm", "cmte_tp",
        "name", "transaction_dt", "transaction_amt",
        "purpose", "cycle",
    ])
    .collect()
    .to_pandas()
)
oppexp_raw["transaction_dt"] = pd.to_datetime(oppexp_raw["transaction_dt"])
oppexp_raw["transaction_amt"] = pd.to_numeric(oppexp_raw["transaction_amt"], errors="coerce")
oppexp_raw["cycle"] = pd.to_numeric(oppexp_raw["cycle"], errors="coerce").astype("Int64")

print(f"loaded {len(oppexp_raw):,} real expenditure records")
print(f"cycles: {sorted(oppexp_raw['cycle'].unique())}")
print(f"total spend: ${oppexp_raw['transaction_amt'].sum():,.0f}")
print(f"\nsample rows (FEC bulk format, post-join):")
print(oppexp_raw[['cmte_nm', 'name', 'transaction_dt', 'transaction_amt', 'purpose']]
      .head().to_string(index=False))

title: Political Media Spend Patterns in FEC Data
text:
Step 2: Categorize free-text spend purposes
The FEC schema has a purpose field, but it’s free-text entered by the committee treasurers with no standardization. Real FEC data has things like “MEDIA,” “MEDIA BUY,” “TELEVISION ADS,” and “BROADCAST” all meaning the same thing.
A rule-based classifier handles this reliably without ML overhead:


Rule-based spend categorization
import re

### categorization rules: (regex pattern, category)
### Order matters — more specific rules first
CATEGORY_RULES = [
    (r'(?<![A-Z0-9])(?:TV|T\.V\.|T\.V|TELEVISION|BROADCAST|CABLE|MEDIA\s+BUY)(?![A-Z0-9])','TV/Broadcast'),
    (r'(?<![A-Z0-9])(?:DIGITAL|ONLINE|SOCIAL\s+MEDIA|WEBSITE|FACEBOOK|GOOGLE)(?![A-Z0-9])', 'Digital'),
    (r'(?<![A-Z0-9])(?:DIRECT\s+MAIL|POSTAGE|PRINTING|MAIL\s+PRODUCTION)(?![A-Z0-9])', 'Direct Mail'),
    (r'(?<![A-Z0-9])(?:POLLING|SURVEY|FOCUS\s+GROUP|RESEARCH)(?![A-Z0-9])', 'Polling/Research'),
    (r'(?<![A-Z0-9])CONSULT\w*','Consulting'),
    (r'(?<![A-Z0-9])(?:PAYROLL|SALARY|OFFICE|RENT|TRAVEL|EVENT)(?![A-Z0-9])','Operations')]


def categorize(text):
    text = str(text).upper()

    for pattern, category in CATEGORY_RULES:
        if re.search(pattern, text):
            return category

    return 'Other'


oppexp_raw['category'] = oppexp_raw['purpose'].fillna('').apply(categorize)

## honest coverage numbers, the key results section reads these at render time
rec_cov = (oppexp_raw['category'] != 'Other').mean() * 100
named = oppexp_raw.loc[oppexp_raw['category'] != 'Other', 'transaction_amt'].sum()
dol_cov = named / oppexp_raw['transaction_amt'].sum() * 100

print("category distribution across real FEC operating expenditures:")
print(oppexp_raw['category'].value_counts().to_string())
print(f"\ncoverage: {rec_cov:.0f}% of records and {dol_cov:.0f}% of dollars get a named category")

print("\n example purpose strings the classifier saw (sample of 5 per category):")
for cat in ['TV/Broadcast', 'Digital', 'Direct Mail']:
    sample = (
        oppexp_raw[oppexp_raw['category'] == cat]
        ['purpose'].dropna().unique()[:5]
    )
    print(f"  {cat:<15} -> {list(sample)}")

title: Political Media Spend Patterns in FEC Data
text:
Step 3: The partitioned Parquet warehouse
The right partitioning here is cycle (queries are almost always cycle-bounded) plus month within cycle (supports time-series analysis without too many tiny files). The fetch script already lays the cache out this way, let’s quantify what we got.


Inspect the cycle/month-partitioned warehouse
import os

PARQUET_ROOT = CACHE
all_files = [
    os.path.join(dp, f)
    for dp, _, files in os.walk(PARQUET_ROOT)
    for f in files if f.endswith('.parquet')
]
total_size = sum(os.path.getsize(f) for f in all_files)

## compare against the row-equivalent CSV size for the same data
csv_path = '/tmp/fec_oppexp.csv'
oppexp_raw.to_csv(csv_path, index=False)
csv_size = os.path.getsize(csv_path)

## key results reads this at render time too
compression_ratio = csv_size / max(total_size, 1)

print(f"Parquet warehouse: {PARQUET_ROOT}")
print(f"  partitions:     {len(all_files)} files (cycle × month)")
print(f"  total size:     {total_size / 1024**2:.2f} MB")
print(f"  csv equivalent: {csv_size / 1024**2:.2f} MB")
print(f"  compression:    {compression_ratio:.1f}× smaller")

title: Political Media Spend Patterns in FEC Data
text:
Step 4: Query with DuckDB
DuckDB on Parquet is the right tool here — full SQL, partition pruning, no infrastructure. Notice how queries that filter on cycle skip entire directories of files.


DuckDB analytical queries
import duckdb

### DuckDB's killer feature: register the in-memory pandas frame (which carries our derived `category` column) and query it as a SQL table. Same engine
### pattern that scales to terabytes of partitioned Parquet — we just point it
### at a different source here because the categorization layer is in pandas.
con = duckdb.connect()
con.register("oppexp", oppexp_raw)

channel_by_cycle = con.sql("""
    SELECT
        cycle,
        category,
        SUM(transaction_amt) AS total_spend,
        COUNT(*) AS n_transactions,
        AVG(transaction_amt) AS avg_transaction
    FROM oppexp
    GROUP BY cycle, category
    ORDER BY cycle, total_spend DESC
""").df()

def fmt_currency(x):
    return f"${x:,.0f}"

display = channel_by_cycle.assign(
    total_spend     = channel_by_cycle["total_spend"].map(fmt_currency),
    avg_transaction = channel_by_cycle["avg_transaction"].map(fmt_currency),
    n_transactions  = channel_by_cycle["n_transactions"].map(lambda v: f"{v:,}"),
)
print("Channel spend by election cycle:")
print(display.to_string(index=False))

  2024      Direct Mail   $735,031,582         90,693          $8,105

Top vendors by category — the kind of question stakeholders ask
## light entity resolution before ranking. raw FEC names split the same firm
## into multiple rows: "RWT PRODUCTION LLC" vs "RWT PRODUCTION, LLC" vs
## "MEDIA BUYING & ANALYTICS" vs "MEDIA BUYING & ANALYTICS LLC"
### kill commas and periods, collapse space runs, drop trailing LLC / INC / etc,
### then title case. has to happen BEFORE the group by so the dollars combine
v = oppexp_raw['name'].fillna('').str.upper()
v = (v.str.replace(r'[.,]', ' ', regex=True)
      .str.replace(r'\s+', ' ', regex=True)
      .str.strip())
v = v.str.replace(r'(?:\s+(?:LLC|L L C|INC|LTD|LLP|CORP|CO|PC))+$', '', regex=True)
oppexp_raw['vendor_clean'] = v.str.title()

## re-register so duckdb sees the new column
con.register("oppexp", oppexp_raw)

top_vendors = con.sql("""
    SELECT
        category,
        vendor_clean AS vendor,
        SUM(transaction_amt) AS total_spend,
        COUNT(*) AS n_transactions
    FROM oppexp
    WHERE category IN ('TV/Broadcast', 'Digital', 'Direct Mail')
    GROUP BY category, vendor_clean
    QUALIFY ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY category ORDER BY total_spend DESC) <= 3
    ORDER BY category, total_spend DESC
""").df()

display_vendors = top_vendors.assign(
    total_spend    = top_vendors["total_spend"].map(fmt_currency),
    n_transactions = top_vendors["n_transactions"].map(lambda v: f"{v:,}"),
)
print("Top 3 vendors per major paid-media category:")
print(display_vendors.to_string(index=False))
