title: Political Media Spend Patterns in FEC Data
text:
Step 5: Executive-ready visualization
Now the part stakeholders actually see. A chart is exec ready when the title states the finding, the labels sit on the data instead of in a legend box, and each chart makes exactly one point. Everything below follows those three rules.


Shared chart style and helpers
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import matplotlib.ticker as mtick

## one palette for the whole piece. navy carries TV, blue is digital,
## slate fades mail into the background. red is reserved for election day
## so it always means the same thing
NAVY, BLUE, SLATE, RED, GRAY = '#0f172a', '#2563eb', '#94a3b8', '#dc2626', '#64748b'
colors = {'TV/Broadcast': NAVY, 'Digital': BLUE, 'Direct Mail': SLATE}

plt.rcParams.update({
    'axes.spines.top': False,
    'axes.spines.right': False,
    'figure.facecolor': 'white',
    'font.size': 10,
})

def fmt_dollars(v):
    ### $1.2B above a billion, $850M below
    return f'${v/1e9:.1f}B' if v >= 1e9 else f'${v/1e6:.0f}M'

def headline(ax, title, sub):
    ### takeaway goes big and bold, the literal description goes small and gray
    ax.set_title(title, fontsize=14, fontweight='bold', loc='left', pad=30, color=NAVY)
    ax.text(0, 1.06, sub, transform=ax.transAxes, fontsize=9.5, color=GRAY)


First view: the size of the pie. Every bar gets a dollar label so the y axis can go away entirely, nobody has to squint and interpolate.


Total paid media spend by cycle
## filter to paid media for a cleaner story
paid_media = ['TV/Broadcast', 'Digital', 'Direct Mail']
plot_data = channel_by_cycle[channel_by_cycle['category'].isin(paid_media)].copy()

## share of paid media within each cycle, the slope chart uses this too
totals = plot_data.groupby('cycle')['total_spend'].transform('sum')
plot_data['share'] = plot_data['total_spend'] / totals * 100

cycles = sorted(plot_data['cycle'].unique())
x = np.arange(len(cycles))
width = 0.27

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 4.6))

for i, cat in enumerate(paid_media):
    vals = (plot_data[plot_data['category'] == cat]
            .set_index('cycle').reindex(cycles)['total_spend'])
    bars = ax.bar(x + (i - 1) * width, vals / 1e6, width,
                  label=cat, color=colors[cat])
    ## dollar label on every bar
    for rect, v in zip(bars, vals):
        ax.text(rect.get_x() + rect.get_width() / 2, rect.get_height() + 45,
                fmt_dollars(v), ha='center', fontsize=9.5,
                fontweight='bold', color=colors[cat])

ax.set_xticks(x)
ax.set_xticklabels([f"{c}\n{'midterm' if c % 4 else 'presidential'}" for c in cycles])

### labels carry the values, so the whole y axis can go
ax.yaxis.set_visible(False)
ax.spines['left'].set_visible(False)
ax.margins(y=0.12)

ax.legend(fontsize=9, loc='upper right', frameon=False, ncol=1)

headline(ax, 'TV is still the biggest line item, every cycle',
         'total paid media disbursements by election cycle, FEC operating expenditures')

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()





Paid media disbursements by election cycle. TV holds the top spot in all three cycles despite a decade of digital growth headlines.




Second view: the mix. The stacked bar version of this chart made readers do arithmetic to see movement. A slope chart shows the movement directly, and the midterm anomaly becomes the headline.


Channel mix shift across cycles, slope chart
mix = plot_data.pivot(index='cycle', columns='category', values='share')[paid_media]

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 4.6))

## soft band behind the midterm column, that's where the action is
ax.axvspan(2021.7, 2022.3, color='#f1f5f9', zorder=0)

## value labels go above the line for TV and digital, below for mail,
## otherwise the 2022 numbers pile up on each other
offset = {'TV/Broadcast': 2.2, 'Digital': 2.2, 'Direct Mail': -3.6}

for cat in paid_media:
    ax.plot(mix.index, mix[cat], marker='o', markersize=7, linewidth=2.5,
            color=colors[cat], markerfacecolor='white', markeredgewidth=2.5)
    for cyc in mix.index:
        v = mix.loc[cyc, cat]
        ax.text(cyc, v + offset[cat], f'{v:.0f}%', ha='center',
                fontsize=10, fontweight='bold', color=colors[cat])
    ## channel name rides at the end of its own line, no legend needed
    ax.text(2024.25, mix.loc[2024, cat], cat, va='center',
            fontsize=10, fontweight='bold', color=colors[cat])

ax.set_xticks([2020, 2022, 2024])
ax.set_xlim(2019.5, 2025.9)
ax.set_ylim(5, 56)
ax.yaxis.set_visible(False)
ax.spines['left'].set_visible(False)

headline(ax, 'Midterm money moves to the mailbox',
         'channel share of paid media spend by cycle')

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()





Channel share of paid media across cycles. Direct mail nearly doubles its share in the midterm, then gives most of it back in 2024.

title: Political Media Spend Patterns in FEC Data
section: How spend ramps to election day
text:
How spend ramps to election day
A more interesting analytical view: how does spend ramp toward election day? The hockey stick is the expected part. The detail worth an annotation is what happens in election week itself, TV falls off a cliff while digital keeps buying right through it.


Weekly spend pattern leading up to election day
# Compute days to election day for each cycle
election_days = {2020: '2020-11-03', 2022: '2022-11-08', 2024: '2024-11-05'}

oppexp_raw['election_day'] = oppexp_raw['cycle'].map(lambda c: pd.Timestamp(election_days[c]))
oppexp_raw['days_to_election'] = (oppexp_raw['election_day'] - oppexp_raw['transaction_dt']).dt.days
oppexp_raw['weeks_to_election'] = (oppexp_raw['days_to_election'] // 7)

## last 32 weeks before election, paid media only
recent = oppexp_raw[
    (oppexp_raw['weeks_to_election'].between(0, 32)) &
    (oppexp_raw['category'].isin(paid_media))
].copy()

weekly = (
    recent.groupby(['weeks_to_election', 'category'])['transaction_amt']
    .sum().reset_index()
)

## the number that earns the headline: how much of the 32 week window
## lands in the final 8 weeks
last8 = weekly.loc[weekly['weeks_to_election'] <= 8, 'transaction_amt'].sum()
pct_last8 = last8 / weekly['transaction_amt'].sum() * 100

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 5))

## shade the home stretch so the eye goes there first
ax.axvspan(0, 8, color='#fef2f2', zorder=0)

for cat in paid_media:
    cat_data = weekly[weekly['category'] == cat].sort_values('weeks_to_election')
    ax.plot(cat_data['weeks_to_election'], cat_data['transaction_amt'] / 1e6,
            marker='o', markersize=4, linewidth=2, label=cat, color=colors[cat])

ax.axvline(x=0, color=RED, linestyle='--', linewidth=2, alpha=0.7)

## digital is the only channel still peaking in election week, call it out
dig_final = weekly.loc[
    (weekly['category'] == 'Digital') & (weekly['weeks_to_election'] == 0),
    'transaction_amt'
].iloc[0] / 1e6
ax.annotate('digital keeps buying\nthrough election week',
            xy=(0.4, dig_final), xytext=(13, dig_final + 55),
            fontsize=9.5, color=BLUE, fontweight='bold',
            arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle='->', color=BLUE, lw=1.2,
                            connectionstyle='arc3,rad=-0.2'))

ax.text(6, ax.get_ylim()[1] * 0.97, 'final 8 weeks', ha='center', va='top',
        fontsize=9.5, color=RED, alpha=0.8, fontweight='bold')
ax.text(1.1, 12, 'election day', rotation=90, va='bottom',
        fontsize=9, color=RED, alpha=0.8)

ax.set_xlabel('Weeks to election', fontsize=10)
ax.set_ylabel('Weekly spend ($M, all cycles combined)', fontsize=10)
ax.yaxis.set_major_formatter(mtick.FuncFormatter(lambda x, p: f'${x:.0f}M'))

### show 32 weeks out through election week, more tick detail near the end
ax.set_xlim(32, 0)
xticks = [32, 28, 24, 20, 16, 12, 8, 4, 2, 0]
ax.set_xticks(xticks)
ax.set_xticklabels([str(x) for x in xticks])
ax.margins(y=0.08)

ax.legend(fontsize=9, loc='upper left', bbox_to_anchor=(0.03, 1.01), ncol=3,
          frameon=False, columnspacing=1.2, handlelength=2.0, borderaxespad=0)
ax.grid(axis='y', linestyle=':', alpha=0.4)

headline(ax, f'{pct_last8:.0f}% of the money moves in the final 8 weeks',
         'weekly paid media spend by weeks to election, 2020 + 2022 + 2024 combined')

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()





Weekly paid media spend by weeks to election, all three cycles combined. The shaded zone marks the final 8 weeks.

title: Political Media Spend Patterns in FEC Data
text:
Who actually gets the checks
The vendor table from Step 4 answers the question, but a chart makes the concentration visible. A handful of firms clear most of the paid media money.


Top vendors per channel, lollipop chart
## lollipops read better than bars when the labels are long vendor names
## names arrive already cleaned and title cased from the step 4 query
vend = top_vendors.copy()

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 5.4))

y = 0
yticks, ylabels = [], []
for cat in paid_media:
    grp = vend[vend['category'] == cat].sort_values('total_spend')
    ## channel name sits above its group, colored to match
    ax.text(0, y + len(grp) - 0.45, cat, fontsize=10.5,
            fontweight='bold', color=colors[cat])
    for _, r in grp.iterrows():
        v = r['total_spend'] / 1e6
        ax.hlines(y, 0, v, color=colors[cat], linewidth=2, alpha=0.55)
        ax.plot(v, y, 'o', markersize=8, color=colors[cat])
        ax.text(v + vend['total_spend'].max() / 1e6 * 0.02, y,
                fmt_dollars(r['total_spend']), va='center',
                fontsize=9.5, fontweight='bold', color=colors[cat])
        yticks.append(y)
        ylabels.append(r['vendor'])
        y += 1
    y += 1.6   ## gap between channel groups

ax.set_yticks(yticks)
ax.set_yticklabels(ylabels, fontsize=9.5)
ax.set_xlim(0, vend['total_spend'].max() / 1e6 * 1.15)

### the dollar labels carry the values, x axis adds nothing
ax.xaxis.set_visible(False)
ax.spines['left'].set_visible(False)
ax.spines['bottom'].set_visible(False)
ax.tick_params(left=False)

headline(ax, 'A short list of firms clears most of the money',
         'top 3 vendors per paid media channel, 2020 + 2022 + 2024 combined')

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()





Top three vendors by total spend within each paid media channel, all cycles combined.




An earlier draft of this chart made the case for entity resolution by accident. “Rwt Production Llc” and “Rwt Production, Llc” showed up as two separate vendors, split by nothing but a comma, and Media Buying & Analytics appeared with and without its “LLC.” The normalization in Step 4 (strip punctuation, collapse spacing, drop corporate suffixes) merges those rows before ranking, which changes the standings. Harder cases like “AND” vs “&” still need the fuzzy matching described in the production section.

title: Political Media Spend Patterns in FEC Data
text:
Key Results


5.6× Storage compression Parquet vs CSV, measured at render


60% Dollars auto-categorized 44% of records, rule based


6.5M Records analyzed 2020 / 2022 / 2024 cycles


Cycle × Month Partition strategy Optimized for filtered queries

title: Political Media Spend Patterns in FEC Data
text:
What this demonstrates
Real public data, end-to-end. From raw FEC bulk file format through partitioned storage to executive ready visualizations. No synthetic shortcuts.
Pragmatic categorization. The free text purpose field is messy. Six rules name a category for 44% of records and 60% of dollars, and everything else stays in an honest Other bucket instead of getting force fitted. The rules have known rough edges too, the CABLE pattern pulls in some office utility bills alongside actual cable buys. The production path is widening the rule set against the biggest Other purposes by dollar volume, not reaching for an ML model.
Partition discipline. Cycle and month are the columns queries actually filter on. Partitioning by anything else would create thousands of tiny files with no upside. This goes to show to know you’re data and what you’re analyzing.

title: Political Media Spend Patterns in FEC Data
text:
What I’d extend in production

Incremental ingestion — schedule weekly pulls of the latest filings rather than re-downloading everything (crontab, task scheduler, jenkins, js7, aws event bridge, google cloud scheduler)
Entity resolution on vendors — same media buyer often appears under slightly different names across filings (“AKPD MESSAGE & MEDIA” vs. “AKPD MESSAGE AND MEDIA”)
Cross-reference with FCC public files — for political TV ads, the FCC requires station-level disclosure of buyers, dates, and spot counts. Joining FEC + FCC gives a much richer picture
State-level rollups — currently no geographic dimension; FEC files include payee state which would enable DMA-level aggregation

title: Political Media Spend Patterns in FEC Data

title: Analysis Playbook
text:
Visualization & Measurement Library

Every entry in this playbook starts with a question someone actually asks in a business — did the campaign change the trajectory? is this lift real or noise? which metric should we track? — and pairs it with the analysis that answers it honestly, the R code that builds it, and the statistical read that goes with it. Where it’s instructive, the wrong chart is shown next to the right one.
All data is synthetic and seeded, engineered to demonstrate exactly the pattern each entry is about.






🚧 In progress — entries are being added across the five categories below.





Category 01 In progress


Trends & Time Series
Trend breaks, seasonality, dynamic baselines, and anomalies — how to show whether a trajectory actually changed, and how to separate signal from ordinary variance.




Category 02 In progress


Comparison
Share shift, mix effects, and before/after reads — comparing groups, periods, and segments without letting the comparison itself mislead.




Category 03 In progress


Distributions & Uncertainty
Why the average lies — showing variance honestly, when the mean hides the story, and how to put uncertainty on a chart without losing the room.




Category 04 In progress


Experiment Readouts
Test vs. control done right — lift with confidence intervals, significance reads, and the difference between “the test won” and “the test probably won.”




Category 05 In progress


KPIs & Metrics
Leading vs. lagging indicators, vanity vs. trackable metrics — how to define a KPI that survives a quarter of scrutiny, and how to present it.

title: Data Foundation
text: Engineering POV

title: Data Foundation
text:
Why this page exists
Most portfolio sites show charts and code, but quietly assume the data fits in a CSV. That worked in 2015. It doesn’t reflect how modern analytics teams actually operate. When you’re analyzing 50GB of Wikipedia traffic or years of FEC filings, your first decision isn’t “which chart library?” — it’s “how do I store this so I don’t reload it from scratch every time?”
This page documents the engineering choices behind the case studies. They’re not glamorous. They’re the foundation everything else sits on.

title: Data Foundation
text:
The decision tree
For each dataset, I work through the same sequence:



1


What’s the access pattern?
Is this data read once and discarded, or queried repeatedly? Are queries time-bounded (last 30 days), entity-bounded (one client), or full-table scans? The right answer shapes everything downstream.




2


What’s the natural partition key?
Almost always date or time. Sometimes a categorical (DMA, client_id). Partitioning by the column you filter on most reduces query cost by orders of magnitude.




3


What’s the right format?
CSV for tiny exploratory data. Parquet for anything you’ll query more than twice. JSON only when the schema is genuinely heterogeneous. Never, ever Excel as a system of record.




4


What’s the right query engine?
DuckDB for ad-hoc SQL on local Parquet. Polars for transformations. Athena/BigQuery for cloud-scale. Pandas only if the data is small enough that it doesn’t matter.

title: Data Foundation
text:
Why Parquet, specifically
Three reasons that compound:
Columnar storage means column-selective queries are cheap. When you read a CSV with 50 columns and only need 3, you’re still parsing all 50. Parquet stores each column contiguously, so reading 3 columns is genuinely 6% of the work, not 100%.
Compression is dramatic and free. Real numbers from the case studies on this site:









Dataset
CSV size
Parquet size
Ratio




Wikipedia hourly pageviews (1 month)
6.2 GB
412 MB
15× smaller


FEC operating expenditures (2024 cycle)
1.4 GB
96 MB
14× smaller


CMS Open Payments (general payments, 1 yr)
2.1 GB
178 MB
12× smaller



That’s not just disk — it’s network transfer time, S3 storage cost, and memory pressure during reads. All compounding.
Schema is part of the file. No more “wait, is date a string or a timestamp?” The Parquet file knows. Pandas, Polars, DuckDB, Athena, Spark — they all read the same file the same way. No parsing surprises.
The cost: Parquet is binary. You can’t cat it or open it in a text editor. That’s a fair trade for everything you get back.

title: Data Foundation
text:
Partitioning strategy
This is where most teams get it wrong. The instinct is to partition by everything possible (“partition by year, month, day, hour, dma, channel…”). The reality: over-partitioning is worse than under-partitioning because the metadata overhead destroys query performance.
The rule I use:

Partition by what you filter on. Don’t partition by what you only group by.
The single most useful partitioning heuristic.

For time-series data: partition by date. That’s it. Most queries are “last N days” or “this month” — date partitioning makes those skip entire directories of files. Adding hour-level partitioning means thousands of tiny files, each with overhead. Not worth it unless you genuinely run hour-bounded queries.
For mixed dimensions: partition by the high-cardinality column you filter on. For ad-tech data with client_id, partition by client. For multi-tenant data with region, partition by region. Combine with date if the volume justifies it.
The directory structure ends up looking like:
data/
├── wikipedia_pageviews/
│   ├── date=2024-01-01/
│   │   └── part-0.parquet
│   ├── date=2024-01-02/
│   │   └── part-0.parquet
│   └── ...
├── fec_expenditures/
│   ├── cycle=2022/
│   │   ├── month=01/
│   │   │   └── part-0.parquet
│   │   └── month=02/...
│   └── cycle=2024/...
└── cms_payments/
    ├── year=2023/
    │   └── part-0.parquet
    └── year=2024/
        └── part-0.parquet
This is Hive-style partitioning — the directory names encode the partition values. Every modern engine (DuckDB, Polars, Athena, Spark) auto-detects this layout.

title: Data Foundation
text:
The query layer
For ad-hoc analysis: DuckDB on Parquet is the most underrated combination in modern analytics. You get full SQL, partition pruning, column pruning, and zero infrastructure. It runs on a laptop and queries 50GB of Parquet faster than most cloud warehouses query a table that size.
import duckdb

# Query directly against Parquet — no loading step
result = duckdb.sql("""
    SELECT
        date,
        SUM(views) AS total_views,
        COUNT(DISTINCT article) AS articles
    FROM 'data/wikipedia_pageviews/*/*.parquet'
    WHERE date BETWEEN '2024-01-01' AND '2024-01-31'
      AND project = 'en.wikipedia'
    GROUP BY date
    ORDER BY date
""").df()
The WHERE date BETWEEN ... clause causes DuckDB to skip every partition outside that range — it never even opens those files. Same query against the equivalent CSV would take 50–100× longer.
For transformations: Polars. Same lazy-evaluation model as Spark, single-machine execution, written in Rust, ~5–10× faster than pandas on real workloads. The API is more disciplined than pandas (which I view as a feature, not a bug).
import polars as pl

df = (
    pl.scan_parquet('data/wikipedia_pageviews/*/*.parquet')
    .filter(pl.col('project') == 'en.wikipedia')
    .filter(pl.col('date').is_between('2024-01-01', '2024-01-31'))
    .group_by('date')
    .agg([
        pl.col('views').sum().alias('total_views'),
        pl.col('article').n_unique().alias('articles'),
    ])
    .sort('date')
    .collect()
)
scan_parquet is the lazy version — Polars builds an execution plan, applies predicate pushdown to the Parquet files (so only matching row-groups are read), and only materializes the result when .collect() is called.

title: Data Foundation
section: What this signals to a hiring manager
text:
What this signals to a hiring manager
I’m direct about why this page exists. For a Manager or Director-level analytics role, the question isn’t “can you write a SQL query?” It’s “do you make decisions like an engineer when the situation calls for it?”
Showing the storage layer signals three things:

I think in production terms. Cost, latency, and reproducibility matter, not just the final chart.
I make tool choices deliberately. Polars, DuckDB, Parquet aren’t on this page because they’re trendy. They’re here because they’re the right choice for the access patterns I described.
I can lead a team that operates at this level. A team that ships code-driven reports needs an architecture this team can stand on. That architecture is an analytics leadership responsibility, not a “let the data engineering team handle it” handoff.

title: Data Foundation
text:
Honest trade-offs
I want to be clear about what this approach isn’t good for:

Real-time / streaming. Parquet is batch. If you need sub-minute freshness, this is the wrong layer. (You’d add Kafka + a streaming engine on top.)
Frequent row-level updates. Parquet files are immutable; updating individual rows means rewriting partitions. For mostly-append-only data (which describes most analytics workloads), this is fine.
Tiny datasets. If your data fits comfortably in a CSV that opens in Excel, just use a CSV. The infrastructure overhead isn’t justified.
Cross-org data sharing. Parquet works in any modern engine, but if your stakeholders open everything in Excel, you’ll still need to export to xlsx for delivery. (Which is fine — generate it from the Parquet source.)

The point isn’t that Parquet/Polars/DuckDB is universally correct. The point is that making the choice deliberately, and being able to explain why, is what separates senior analytics work from junior analytics work.
